title: Krachunova v. Bulgaria, no. 18269/18, 28 November 2023
text:
The victim is a Bulgarian national who, in April 2012, left her home after a fight with her parents to live with X, his partner, and his four children, in the village of Novachene, about 70 km from Sofia. X's main occupation was driving sex workers to and from work and he was known to the police for being an associate of pimps. As the victim needed money, she engaged in sex work, hoping she "would be able to earn as much as the other girls". By May 2012 she was working every day on the Sofia ring road. By July of that year, she wanted to quit, but alleged that X had beaten and threatened her. In August that year she ran away to her village.

 

X found her, took her back to his house and retained her identity card. She returned to sex work as she felt she had no choice. From that time on, X took all her earnings, buying her just her necessities and giving her pocket money. In February 2013, the victim was arrested. X was summoned to the police station, where he handed over her identity card (which he had on him), claiming that he had been keeping it for her to avoid it being stolen. The police opened an investigation against X.

 

The investigation led to criminal proceedings against X, which the victim joined as private prosecutor and sought to lodge claims for pecuniary and non-pecuniary damages against him. In the first trial, X was found guilty of trafficking in human beings and of inciting the victim to engage in prostitution, and he was ordered to pay the victim compensation for non-pecuniary damage. The claim for pecuniary damage, however, was withdrawn by the victim after the court determined that it could not be allowed as the money involved had been earned through immoral acts. The victim appealed this decision, and the appellate court found the matter had to be re-examined and re-trialled.

 

In the re-trial, the victim once again sought to lodge claims for compensation for non-pecuniary and pecuniary damages. The court admitted the claim for non-pecuniary damage but dismissed the one for pecuniary damage, as it concerned immoral acts. X was only found guilty of trafficking in human beings, a charge which, according to the court, already encompassed inciting the victim to engage in prostitution. In the subsequent appeal by the victim against the dismissal of her claim for pecuniary damage, the upper court fully agreed with this decision and found that claim as unfounded.

 

The victim brought the matter to the European Court of Human Rights, alleging that, by dismissing her claim for compensation for pecuniary damage against her trafficker, the Bulgarian courts had been in breach of Article 4 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms. The Court held that Article 4 effectively includes the positive obligation for States to enable victims of trafficking to seek compensation for lost earnings against her trafficker, which the victim in this case had been unable to do. Thus, the Court found that, by dismissing the victim's claim, the decisions of Bulgarian courts had led to a violation of Article 4.

 

Aside from costs and expenses, the Court ordered Bulgaria to pay the victim 6000 EUR as compensation for non-pecuniary damage for the mental suffering provoked by the dismissal of her claim but dismissed the claim for pecuniary damage presented to it, because, the Court argued, it could not pronounce itself on whether, if initially admitted, the claim for pecuniary damage would have been considered well-founded by the Bulgarian courts and if they would have agreed with the amount sought.
